name: Belgian Pro League
content:
The Belgian Pro League is the highest level of professional football in Belgium. It was established in 1895 by the Royal Belgian Football Association and has evolved into the country’s top-tier competition. The league is officially known as the Jupiler Pro League due to sponsorship reasons, and it represents the first division in the Belgian football pyramid. It features leading clubs such as RSC Anderlecht, Club Brugge, and Standard Liège, which have historically dominated the competition.

Over time, the Belgian top flight has undergone several structural and naming changes. Before being called the Pro League, it was known as the Belgian First Division A. Below it is the second tier, currently called the Challenger Pro League, which was previously known as the First Division B and even earlier as the Belgian Second Division until its reform in 2016. These changes were part of broader restructuring efforts to modernize the league system and improve competitiveness.

The league currently consists of 16 teams (with plans for expansion in future seasons) and operates a regular season followed by a playoff system that determines the champion, European qualification spots, and relegation. Over its long history, it has remained Belgium’s premier competition and a key contributor to European football, with Belgian clubs regularly participating in UEFA Champions League and Europa competitions.

name: Belgian Cup
content:
The Belgian Cup (French: Coupe de Belgique, Dutch: Beker van België) is Belgium’s main national knockout football competition. It was first introduced in 1908, initially involving provincial selections under the name Belgian Provinces Cup, before evolving into a competition for club teams from 1912 onward. Since 1964, the tournament has been held annually in its modern format and is organized by the Royal Belgian Football Association. Today it is also commonly known as the Croky Cup due to sponsorship naming rights.

Over the years, the competition has had several official and commercial names. Before becoming the Belgian Cup, it was referred to as the King’s Cup in its early pre–World War I period. In the modern era, sponsorship has played a major role in naming: it was called the Coca-Cola Cup (1996–2001), the Cofidis Cup (2008–2015), and since 2015 it has been known as the Croky Cup. Despite these naming changes, the tournament’s format and prestige as Belgium’s primary cup competition have remained consistent.

The Belgian Cup is a straight knockout tournament open to clubs from multiple levels of Belgian football, from professional sides to amateur teams. The winner not only earns the trophy but also qualifies for the UEFA Europa League and the Belgian Super Cup. The final is traditionally held at the King Baudouin Stadium in Brussels, making it one of the key annual events in Belgian football.
